mnemonics.title: DNS Query Flow
mnemonics.content: Root Asks TLD: 'R-A-T' - Remember: Root → Authoritative → TLD (flow goes through hierarchy: Root directs to TLD, TLD directs to Authoritative)

acronyms.usage: Translates domain names to IP addresses
Duration for which DNS records are cached
Complete domain name ending with a dot (e.g., www.example.com.)
Adds cryptographic authentication to DNS responses
DNS record for mail server configuration
DNS record with zone administration details
DNS record delegating authority to name servers
DNS record creating an alias for another domain

visualTips.visualization: Imagine an inverted tree: Root at top (.) → branches are TLDs (.com, .org) → leaves are Authoritative servers → clients connect at the bottom through resolvers
Think of it like asking for directions: Client asks Resolver → Resolver asks Root → Root points to .com → .com points to authoritative → Authoritative gives IP → Resolver returns to Client

Use a building analogy: A=Address (where you live), AAAA=Big Address (mansion for IPv6), CNAME=Nickname (calls you by another name), MX=Mailbox (where mail goes), NS=Office Manager (delegates), SOA=Boss (has authority info)
Think of a phone's contact list - it remembers numbers so you don't look them up every time. TTL is like setting an expiry date on that memory.

analogies.analogy: DNS is like a phonebook for the internet - you look up a name (google.com) and get the number (IP address) to connect.
Like postal system: Root = Country code office → TLD = State sorting center → Authoritative = Local delivery office → You (resolver) deliver to home.
Recursive is like calling a personal assistant who does all the legwork. Iterative is like calling different departments yourself, each gives you the next number to call.
CNAME is like having a nickname - when someone asks for your nickname, you tell them your real name, and they find your number using that.
TTL is like the expiry date on milk - it tells resolvers how long to 'keep' the information fresh before getting a fresh copy.
